text: Endurance athletes should consume approximately 1.8 g/kg body mass per day of protein to support recovery and training adaptation.
Habitual protein intake in male and female endurance athletes is typically around 1.5 g/kg body mass per day, which falls below the recommended level.
During periods of carbohydrate-restricted training and on rest days, protein requirements for endurance athletes may be elevated beyond 2.0 g/kg body mass per day.
Co-ingesting protein with carbohydrate before or during endurance exercise does not confer a performance advantage when carbohydrate recommendations are already met.
Co-ingesting protein with carbohydrate does not facilitate resynthesis of liver or muscle glycogen stores during recovery when adequate carbohydrate is consumed.
Protein nutrition may optimize the adaptive metabolic response to endurance training under conditions of low carbohydrate and/or energy availability.
Optimal per-meal protein doses for endurance athletes remain undetermined due to insufficient dose-response studies of muscle protein synthesis in relation to endurance exercise.
